title: Chapter 3: The Cyber Party
content: The bass hits me before the door does.

It travels up through the soles of my boots, a low-frequency pulse that rearranges something in my chest cavity. The valet — a kid with bleached eyebrows and a waistcoat — waves me toward the entrance without checking a list. There is no list. There are five hundred people inside Cliff House tonight, and the only credential that matters is showing up.
I show up with my camera.

The glass walls that seemed so austere two days ago are alive now — projection surfaces rippling with generative art, cascading fractals that bloom and collapse in time with the music. The infinity pool has become a holographic ocean. Waves made of light lap at the edges, and beneath the surface, bioluminescent creatures spiral through depths that don't exist. A whale shadows beneath a floating bar, its eye the size of a dinner plate, watching the crowd with the patient sadness of something enormous and made of code.

Inside, AI-generated jellyfish drift through the living room. They pulse with colors I don't have names for — not quite violet, not quite infrared — trailing tendrils that pass through shoulders and champagne flutes without disturbing either. Someone's TikToker is crying underneath one, mascara bleeding, whispering to her phone that it's so beautiful, so beautiful, and I'm filming because that's what I do, I film the spectacle and the people broken open by it.

The smell hits in layers: champagne first, sharp and citric, then vape smoke curling in cotton candy clouds, then someone's Byredo perfume cutting through all of it like a blade through silk. I catch a flute from a passing tray. The bubbles break against my tongue, cold and mineral, and I swallow the fizz and keep moving.

This is what I came for. The anthropology of the ultra-wealthy in their natural habitat. The K-pop idol near the DJ booth, bodyguards forming a discreet perimeter. Two senators' kids doing shots off the pool's holographic surface, their laughter swallowed by the thrum. A venture capitalist in the bathroom mirror, white powder on his pinky nail, his eyes meeting mine in the reflection with the glazed entitlement of someone who knows the rules don't apply. I capture it all. I'm not here to judge. I'm here to document.

I find Jordan Keeler first — or they find me, materializing at my elbow with the gravity-defying ease of someone who lives in the spaces between conversations. They're wearing a mesh shirt over a bandeau, sheer enough to see the constellation of freckles across their collarbones. Their smile is architectural, designed to look effortless.
"You made it," Jordan says, and there's something proprietary in the way they say it, like they personally approved my existence.
"You invited me."

Jordan's smile widens. They don't confirm or deny. They're already scanning the room, flitting toward a cluster of tech founders with the hover-and-dart efficiency of a hummingbird. I watch them go. Jordan Keeler knows everyone, touches nothing, and somehow ends up holding the most interesting cards. I file that. I file everything.
Then I find Jax.

She's in the center of the main room, and I mean that literally — the crowd has arranged itself around her in a loose orbital pattern, pulled into her gravity without seeming to notice. She's wearing a white suit with nothing underneath, the lapels framing the hard line of her sternum, and when she laughs — which she does often, generously — it sounds like champagne being poured. She's holding court. A founder here, an editor there, someone who runs something called a decentralized identity protocol. Jax gives each of them exactly as much attention as they need: not a second more, not a second less.
I lift my camera. I focus on her face through the viewfinder, that face that launched a thousand crypto transactions, and I wait.
At 11:47 — I check the timestamp on my phone, the screen cold against my thumb — Jax's gaze drifts.
Toward the window. Toward the bay.
She holds it there for maybe two seconds. Then she's back, laughing at something the founder said, reaching for her drink. But I saw it. And I wait.
Thirty seconds later, it happens again. The same vector. The same window. The same bay.
I stop filming Jax. I follow her sightline.

Through the glass wall, past the holographic ocean dissolving at the pool's edge, past the terrace with its scattered cigarette butts and abandoned drinks — the yacht. The same yacht I saw that first night from my cottage deck. It's moored across the bay, and its deck lights are off, but the green laser grid sweeps across the water between us and it, painting everything in the color of money and poison.
And on the yacht's deck — a woman.

She's standing at the railing in a white dress. That's all I can see from here: the white, the shape of her, the way she's turned toward Cliff House like a compass finding north. The green laser passes over her, and for one rotation of its blade, she's luminous. Made of emerald light. Then the beam sweeps on, and she's just a silhouette again, a cut-out figure against the dark water.
Vivienne.

I don't know her name yet. But I feel the weight of her — the way the whole party seems to be arranged around her absence. The way the laser keeps pulsing across the bay like a heartbeat, like a signal, like someone saying I'm here, I'm here, I'm still here.
I look back at Jax.

And in the glass wall's reflection — the crowd and the holograms and the jellyfish all layered on top of each other — I see Jax's face. Not the face she's been wearing all night, the one built for cameras and handshakes and the right kind of attention. The other face. The one underneath. A woman looking at something she can't have across a stretch of water she can't cross, and the wanting on her features is so raw and so old — not tonight old, not this-year old, something deeper, something measured in the language of waiting — that it makes my chest hurt.
Then it's gone. Jax laughs. Jax charms. Jax is the center again.
But I saw it.

I'm rewinding the footage on my camera when Jax's phone buzzes on the bar. The screen lights up, and I shouldn't look — that's not who I am, I observe public behavior, I don't snoop through notifications — but my eyes catch it anyway. A single message. From a blocked number.
"Stop."

Two letters. Five characters including the period. I don't even have time to process the word before Jax's hand is there, swiping the notification away. But her face — God, her face. It does something I couldn't script if I tried. Pain first, a flinch that collapses the architecture of her expression. Then fury, white-hot, tightening her jaw and narrowing her eyes. Then reset. The whole transformation takes maybe three seconds, and then Jax Vance pockets her phone and turns back to the room with a smile so sharp it could cut the glass walls to ribbons.
She walks into the crowd. She touches an arm, throws her head back in a laugh, accepts a fresh champagne flute. She is immaculate. She is impenetrable. She is entirely rebuilt.
And I caught it on film.
The crack in the myth. The breath before the mask. Three seconds of a woman receiving a wound and deciding to survive it.

I'm standing at the bar, watching Jax move through her kingdom, when Jordan materializes beside me again. They're quieter this time. The architectural smile has been replaced with something more deliberate.
"You noticed."
I don't pretend otherwise. "Hard not to."
Jordan looks at me, and for the first time since I met them, their expression lacks performance. What's left is something careful and conspiring, the look of someone deciding how much truth to lend.
"The woman on the yacht is named Vivienne Sterling," Jordan says. Their voice is low enough that I have to lean in. "She's married to Tom Hargrove."
The name lands with weight. Tom Hargrove. Hargrove Capital. Old money, new influence, the kind of power that doesn't need to announce itself because it's already in the walls.
"And Jax," Jordan continues, "has been looking at that yacht every single night for two years."

Two years. Every night. I do the math. I think about all the geotagged posts at 11:47 PM. I think about the green laser I saw my first night here, pulsing across the bay like it was trying to reach someone. I think about Cliff House, this glass temple on the edge of the continent, built to be seen from the water.
This isn't a party.
It's a signal fire.

Everything Jax built — the crypto empire, the online mythology, the algorithms and the aesthetics and the curated vulnerability — it's not for her followers. It's not for the investors or the influencers or the documentary filmmakers with their cameras and their questions. It's all aimed at one person across the bay. One woman in a white dress standing on the deck of a yacht she doesn't own, in a life she didn't choose.

Jordan's watching me process this, and there's something almost tender in their calculation. They gave me the puzzle piece because they wanted me to have it. Because keeping it to themselves wasn't useful anymore. Jordan Keeler plays the long game, and I just became part of it.
"Why are you telling me?" I ask.
Jordan's smile returns, smaller and stranger than before. "Because someone should know. And you're already looking."
They drift back into the crowd. The jellyfish pulse overhead. The bass rearranges my ribs. I look across the bay at the yacht, but the deck is empty now. The woman in the white dress has gone inside.

The party doesn't end so much as dissolve. Around 3 AM, the projection walls power down, and the glass becomes glass again — cold and transparent and ordinary. The holographic ocean in the pool sputters, glitches, and winks out, leaving only chlorinated water and a few floating lime wedges. The AI jellyfish dissolve into pixels, and the fractals collapse into dark screens. The K-pop idol departed hours ago. The senators' kids are asleep on a daybed, their heads touching like children. The venture capitalist is gone. The cleaning staff has begun their silent, efficient orbit.

I find the beach path by accident, following the stone steps down from the terrace to a sliver of private sand. The night is very still. The bass has been off for thirty minutes, and the silence in my body feels like the quiet after a storm — shocked and grateful and not yet ready to trust it.
Jax is sitting at the waterline.

Her white suit jacket is gone. Her bare feet are in the wet sand, and the tide is touching her toes, retreating, returning, retreating. The green laser still pulses across the water — the only light left. It sweeps over the bay in its slow rotation, and every time it passes, it paints Jax's face in shades of chartreuse and shadow. Beautiful and wrecked. A ruin made of light.
I don't decide to sit beside her. I just do. The sand is cold beneath me. The salt wind comes off the ocean and fills my lungs with brine and distance.

I have my camera. It's on the strap around my neck, the weight of it familiar, the lens cap still off from the last shot I took inside — the senators' kids asleep, a portrait of the aftermath. I could film this. Jax Vance on the beach at 3 AM, her empire dark behind her, the green laser writing its message across the water. It would be the most honest footage I've captured all night.
I set the camera on the sand beside me. The strap pools in the wet darkness. I don't look through the viewfinder.
Some things aren't content.

The waves do their work — pushing, pulling, erasing footprints. The house behind us is completely dark now. No fractals. No jellyfish. Just a glass box on a cliff, empty and waiting, pointed at the bay like an arrow.
Jax doesn't look at me. She looks at the water. At the pulse of green crossing it. At the yacht I can barely see now, a darker shape against the dark.
"You're going to ask if I'm okay," she says. Her voice is different out here. Stripped of the acoustics, the audience, the architecture. Just a voice. Tired and true.
"I'm going to ask who told you to stop."

Jax's head turns. Slowly. The laser sweeps across her face, and in its light I can see everything: the surprise that I saw the message, the calculation of how much I know, the exhaustion of someone who has been holding something for so long that they've forgotten what it feels like to set it down.
She doesn't answer.

The laser pulses. The waves pull at her feet. The yacht rocks on its mooring across the bay, empty and watching. Vivienne's white dress is gone. The deck is dark. But the light keeps reaching, green and steady, across all that impossible water.
Jax turns back toward the bay. I sit in the silence beside her and I understand, now, what the green laser is. Not art. Not ambiance. Not an aesthetic choice.
A lighthouse.
And Jax Vance is the shore that built it, burning and burning and burning, hoping that someday the ship will turn around.
My camera sits in the sand beside me, lens open to the night sky, recording nothing.
